docs.title: Vulnerable Population Consent Protocol (VPCP)
docs.summary: This protocol applies to any prospective resident who, at the time of first contact, is in one or more of the following categories:
docs.content:
# Vulnerable Population Consent Protocol (VPCP)

**Status: Active — Governance**

**Purpose:** Define the consent standards that govern how the pilot town recruits residents who are in a position of material vulnerability at the time of first contact. These standards are stricter than the general consent process and cannot be waived by the founding team or by the prospective resident.

---

### VPCP-001 — Scope and Applicability

This protocol applies to any prospective resident who, at the time of first contact, is in one or more of the following categories:

- Experiencing homelessness or housing insecurity (including couch-surfing, shelter stays, or imminent eviction)
- Unemployed for 90 or more consecutive days
- Released from incarceration within the prior 24 months
- Fleeing domestic violence or human trafficking
- Aging out of foster care (age 18–25)
- In active addiction recovery (including participation in treatment programs, medication-assisted treatment, or peer recovery support)
- Receiving means-tested public benefits as primary income support (SNAP, SSI, SSDI, TANF, Medicaid, Section 8/HCV, or state equivalents)

Where a prospective resident meets multiple categories, the most protective provision applicable to each relevant category governs. The founding team may not argue that one category cancels out another.

A prospective resident outside VPCP-001 scope who enters the pilot and later discloses a qualifying condition must be offered VPCP protections retroactively from the point of disclosure.

---

### VPCP-002 — Pre-Offer Stability Requirement

Before any formal offer of residency is made to a person in VPCP-001 scope, all three of the following must be complete and delivered to the prospective resident in writing:

**Unconditional transitional support pre-arrangement.** Ninety days of transitional support — covering housing, food, and healthcare continuity — must be pre-arranged and funded regardless of whether the person joins the pilot. This support is not contingent on continued interest in joining, on good behavior, or on any other condition. The founding team must document the funding source, the service provider, and the person's confirmed access before making the offer.

**Benefits impact assessment.** A written analysis must be produced showing which public benefits the person currently receives, which they may lose or risk upon joining the pilot (with timeline and estimated dollar amounts), and what steps, if any, can mitigate that loss. This document must be written in plain language (6th-grade reading level or below) and translated into the person's primary language if it differs from English.

**Alternatives presentation.** At least three realistic alternative pathways to stable housing or support must be identified and presented in writing. These must be actual available options — not theoretical programs — with contact information and the founding team's honest assessment of accessibility. The purpose is to demonstrate that the person is not being presented with a false binary between joining the pilot and having nothing.

The founding team must not make the offer until all three deliverables are complete and confirmed received by the prospective resident.

---

### VPCP-003 — Independent Consent Advocate (ICA)

**Appointment.** The ICA is not appointed by the founding team. ICA staffers are nominated by a civil-society panel composed of organizations with no financial interest in the pilot (examples: legal aid organizations, housing nonprofits, formerly incarcerated persons' advocacy groups, domestic violence service providers). The founding team funds the ICA but has no authority over hiring, assignment, or findings.

**Staffing requirement.** At least 50% of ICA capacity serving VPCP-scope residents must be staffed by trained peer specialists — people with lived experience of homelessness, incarceration, addiction recovery, domestic violence, foster care aging-out, or the specific vulnerability category relevant to the resident. Peer specialist training must meet a recognized standard (e.g., state certification or equivalent).

**ICA role.** The ICA meets with the prospective resident privately, without founding team members present, before and during the cooling-off period. The ICA explains the offer, answers questions, identifies risks the resident may not have considered, and gives the resident a direct channel to raise concerns. The ICA does not advocate for the pilot; they advocate for the prospective resident's informed autonomy.

**Adverse findings.** The ICA has authority to issue an adverse finding if it determines that consent conditions are not being met for a category of prospective residents. An adverse finding immediately pauses all recruitment outreach targeting that VPCP-001 category until a 14-day civil-society response period completes. The founding team must respond in writing to any adverse finding within 14 days. If the founding team disagrees with the finding, the dispute is referred to the adversarial panel member on the keyholder council, whose determination is binding. ICA findings, founding team responses, and keyholder council determinations are public documents.

---

### VPCP-004 — Cooling-Off Period

After all VPCP-002 materials have been delivered and the ICA has completed at least one session with the prospective resident, a mandatory 30-day cooling-off period begins.

During the cooling-off period:

- No commitment documents may be presented, signed, or executed.
- The founding team may not initiate contact with the prospective resident. The founding team may respond to questions the resident initiates, and must do so promptly, but may not use responses as an opportunity to promote the offer.
- The prospective resident may withdraw interest at any point during the cooling-off period at no cost, penalty, or loss of the VPCP-002 transitional support.

After the cooling-off period ends, the prospective resident may choose to proceed, extend their consideration, or decline. Declining at any point forfeits no transitional support and creates no obligation.

---

### VPCP-005 — Informed Understanding Verification

Before the prospective resident signs any commitment document, the founding team must verify comprehension using the teach-back method: the resident explains back in their own words what they are agreeing to, what rights they retain, what they are giving up, what the pilot can change and cannot change about their situation, and how they can exit.

Plain-language materials (6th-grade reading level or below) must be provided in the resident's primary language. If professional translation is needed, the founding team pays for it.

Understanding verification is not a gatekeeping test. It is the founding team's obligation to ensure comprehension, not the resident's obligation to pass a test. If a resident cannot demonstrate understanding after reasonable support — additional explanation, different materials, interpreter assistance, more time — the offer is suspended until understanding is achievable. The offer is not declined because of comprehension difficulty; it is held open.

The ICA must be present for or promptly informed of the teach-back session. If the ICA has concerns about the quality of comprehension, it may issue a finding under VPCP-003.

---

### VPCP-006 — Exit Rights (Non-Waivable)

Regardless of what the resident agrees to in any commitment document, the following rights cannot be waived by any party:

- **Housing continuity.** Sixty days of continued access to their current housing unit after declaring exit intent.
- **Healthcare continuity.** Ninety days of continued access to healthcare services after declaring exit intent.
- **Essential Access continuity.** The resident's Survival Floor / Essential Access tier continues for 90 days post-exit, regardless of Tier 1 standing at the time of exit.
- **No financial penalty.** No debt, clawback, or financial penalty for exiting. Residency investments made by the community on behalf of a resident — infrastructure improvements, skill development expenditures, service credits — belong to the community, not the exiting resident, and cannot be converted into exit obligations.
- **Service Record export.** The resident's full Service Record is exported in a portable, machine-readable format within 7 days of exit request. The resident owns their record.
- **Return-to-prior-status support.** If the resident was in VPCP-001 scope upon entry, they are entitled to a 90-day return-to-prior-status support package funded by the pilot. This package assists re-connection with public benefits, housing waitlists, and community support networks that may have been disrupted by participation in the pilot.

These rights are incorporated by reference into every resident agreement. Any agreement term that purports to waive or limit them is void.

---

### VPCP-007 — Ongoing Consent

**Annual re-affirmation.** Each VPCP-scope resident is offered an ICA session annually. The session reviews whether the resident's current situation, understanding of the system, and consent to continued participation remain current. The session is the resident's, not the founding team's — the resident sets the agenda. Declining the annual session is not a problem; the offer must be made.

**Change-triggered review.** Any material change to pilot governance, living conditions, community rules, or Essential Access terms triggers a mandatory ICA session for all VPCP-scope residents within 30 days of the change taking effect. "Material change" is defined broadly: when in doubt, it triggers. The ICA determines whether a change is material; the founding team does not.

**Annual consent health report.** The ICA publishes an annual public report covering: number of VPCP-scope residents enrolled and active, adverse findings issued and their resolution, exit rates by VPCP-001 category, resident-initiated concerns and how they were addressed, and ICA staffing compliance with the peer-specialist requirement. The founding team may not edit or delay this report.

---

### VPCP-008 — Pre-Recruitment Prerequisites

No recruitment outreach targeting VPCP-001 scope populations may begin until all four of the following conditions are met and documented:

- **VPCP-002 support infrastructure is operational.** Transitional support funding and service agreements are in place and verified, not merely planned. Documentation must include executed contracts with service providers and confirmed funding in an escrow or restricted account.
- **ICA is staffed and ready.** At least two ICA staffers are trained, available, and confirmed — including at least one peer specialist. Neither may have been appointed by the founding team.
- **Exit support fund is pre-capitalized.** The fund covers a minimum of 90 days of VPCP-006 benefits (housing, healthcare, Essential Access continuity, and return-to-prior-status support) for the full expected first-cohort size. The fund must be held in a restricted account and cannot be used for other purposes without civil-society panel approval.
- **Founding Legitimacy Dossier consent evidence is at PRODUCED status for VPCP-scope populations.** The general consent evidence artifact must be at PRODUCED status, and the VPCP adds an additional vulnerable-population consent evidence artifact that must separately reach PRODUCED status. This artifact documents that all VPCP-002 through VPCP-007 mechanisms are operational and that the civil-society panel has reviewed and affirmed the ICA appointment process.

---

### Residual Risk and Honest Limitations

This protocol does not fully solve every consent problem that arises when vulnerable people join an intentional community. Four limitations deserve explicit acknowledgment:

**Social pressure from existing residents.** Once inside the community, a new resident encounters people who have made the pilot their home and who have strong social and reputational stakes in its success. That pressure is real and this protocol has no mechanism for it. A resident who wants to leave may face soft discouragement from peers who are not founding team members and who act in good faith but still make exit harder.

**Informal status hierarchies once living inside the community.** The pilot's contribution-tracking system creates visible differences in standing. Even with a Survival Floor guarantee, residents who came in with lower service records may experience informal deference to longer-tenured or higher-contributing residents. These hierarchies can operate on vulnerable new arrivals in ways that constrain voice and exit even when formal rights exist.

**Gratitude-debt.** Residents who arrived from homelessness, incarceration, or other acute distress may feel profound gratitude to the community that gave them stability. Gratitude is not coercion, but it creates a psychological bind — voicing dissent or leaving can feel like betrayal of people who helped you. This protocol cannot dissolve that bind. The best it can do is name it explicitly and make the ICA available as a private channel for processing it.

**Exit rights that exist on paper but are hard to use.** Sixty days of continued housing and 90 days of healthcare are meaningful, but a resident who has disengaged from outside support networks during their time in the pilot may face a steep re-entry cliff even with full use of VPCP-006 rights. The return-to-prior-status package is a partial response, but reconnecting to public benefits, housing waitlists, and community ties takes longer than 90 days for many people. The protocol names this gap honestly rather than pretending the exit rights make leaving easy.

docs.title: Pilot Timeline Framework
docs.status: Status: DRAFT — operational planning document for the founding pilot town (1–5,000 residents, built from scratch).
docs.summary: This document maps the five-track parallel timeline for building the pilot town. It is a planning instrument, not a governance guarantee. The timelines are estimates; the gate dependencies are hard constraints.
docs.content:
# Pilot Timeline Framework

**Status:** DRAFT — operational planning document for the founding pilot town (1–5,000 residents, built from scratch).

This document maps the five-track parallel timeline for building the pilot town. It is a planning instrument, not a governance guarantee. The timelines are estimates; the gate dependencies are hard constraints.

---

## Five-Track Structure

The five tracks run in parallel wherever dependencies allow. Track A (Founding Legitimacy) is the critical path. No other track may pass a major gate until the corresponding Track A gate clears. A track may do preparatory work in advance — but no binding commitments, site closings, construction contracts, or resident enrollments may execute until the relevant Track A gate is confirmed.

The tracks are:

- **Track A — Founding Legitimacy:** constitutional prerequisites, artifact production, and post-activation audit
- **Track B — Site Acquisition:** site search, due diligence, land acquisition, and Community Land Trust establishment
- **Track C — Design and Permitting:** design principles, full site plan, and regulatory permitting
- **Track D — Construction:** infrastructure, residential units, shared facilities, and scale buildout
- **Track E — Recruitment and Pre-Enrollment:** awareness outreach, pre-enrollment, and cohort enrollment

---

## Track A — Founding Legitimacy

Track A governs the constitutional foundation of the pilot. Every major downstream gate depends on it. Rushing Track A to meet a construction schedule is not acceptable — the founding legitimacy claim is either real or it is not.

**A0 — Initiation (Month 0):** Step 1 public consultation begins. The Founding Legitimacy Dossier prerequisite resolution process opens. A 60-day public window opens for comment on the two unresolved prerequisites: the definitions of "non-coercive consent" and "civil society" as applied to the pilot context.

**A1 — Prerequisites Resolved (Month 2):** The 60-day window closes. Definitions of "non-coercive consent" and "civil society" are finalized via public consultation and recorded in the Dossier. Production of the seven pre-activation artifacts may now begin:
1. Conflict register
2. Scope declaration
3. Objection record
4. Civil-society review
5. Consent evidence
6. Exit rehearsal
7. Founder sunset plan

**A2 — Gate A Clears (Month 8):** All seven pre-activation artifacts reach PRODUCED status. This is the primary critical-path gate. No pilot phase depending on founding authority may begin before this gate. This includes: finalizing site acquisition (B2), opening pre-enrollment (E Window 2), and authorizing construction financing draws.

**A3 — Founder Sunset (post-activation):** Governed by Founding Team Composition Standard FS-1. The founder sunset occurs at the earlier of 90 days after full occupancy or 24 months after the first resident takes occupancy. This is not a timeline estimate — it is a constitutional deadline.

**A4 — Gate B (post-activation + 90 days):** Post-activation audit complete. Full founding legitimacy claim available. The pilot may represent itself publicly as having completed the founding legitimacy process only after this gate clears.

---

## Track B — Site Acquisition

Track B may begin preparatory work immediately, but no binding site commitment may be made until Gate A (A2) has cleared or is confirmed imminent.

**B0 — Site Search (Month 0, parallel with A0):** Site Selection Criteria document is used to screen candidate locations. Goal: a shortlist of 3–5 sites by Month 3. Screening criteria include: land area sufficient for 1,000+ residents at full build, legal pathway to CLT structure, environmental suitability, proximity to existing infrastructure, and absence of displacement risk to current residents or land users.

**B1 — Due Diligence (Month 3–6):** Environmental review, legal title analysis, and community consultation for shortlisted sites. No site may be selected that does not satisfy all required characteristics in the Site Selection Criteria document. Community consultation for adjacent populations is required, not optional.

**B2 — Site Secured (Month 7–9, after A2):** Community Land Trust structure established; ground lease framework recorded. This gate requires Gate A to have cleared or to be confirmed imminent (all artifacts at PRODUCED, pending administrative finalization only). The founding team may not close on a site in anticipation of Gate A — the risk of Gate A failing must be priced into the site acquisition decision.

**B3 — Community Land Trust Operational (Month 10–12):** CLT board seated with the required resident representative pathway established. The CLT governance structure must include a clear pathway for future residents to hold board seats as occupancy grows.

---

## Track C — Design and Permitting

**C0 — Design Principles Published (Month 4):** Venus Project-inspired design elements are formalized in a public design document. Core elements: circular layout with community and chapel center, residential rings, production facilities at the perimeter, agricultural integration, and nature corridors throughout. A 30-day community input period follows publication. Design principles may not be finalized until input period closes.

**C1 — Design Complete (Month 12–14, after B2):** Full site plan finalized, including: unit types and counts, shared infrastructure (water, sanitation, power, communications), agricultural and production zones, healthcare facility, faith and community spaces, and emergency access routes. The design must reflect input received during C0 and document how that input was addressed.

**C2 — Permitting Complete (Month 16–18):** All required local, county, and state permits in hand. The Jurisdiction Interface Clause is in effect with governing jurisdictions. The founding team may not break ground until all permits are secured — not "substantially complete" or "expected shortly."

---

## Track D — Construction

**D0 — Groundbreaking (Month 18–20, after C2):** Infrastructure first: water, power, sanitation, and communications. Construction workers during this phase are paid workers, not residents. Payroll is split between external currency and Flow per Founding Capital Framework FC-7. The split ratio and conditions are defined in FC-7; this document does not override them.

**D1 — Core Infrastructure Complete (Month 22–24):** Water, sanitation, power, road access, and emergency access route all operational and inspected. No residential construction may begin until core infrastructure is confirmed complete and functional.

**D2 — First Residential Units Complete (Month 26–30):** Minimum viable residential capacity for Cohort 1: 100–150 units, habitable and inspected. Healthcare facility at minimum viable operation (primary care, emergency stabilization, mental health first response). This gate unlocks E Window 3 (enrollment). It is the second critical-path gate.

**D3 — Full Buildout Phase 1 Complete (Month 36–42):** 300–500 units; full shared facilities operational; agricultural and production zones operational and producing. The earned income economy begins at this phase.

**D4 — Scale Capacity (Month 48–60):** Capacity for 1,000+ residents across all facility types. Shared Storehouse fully operational. Emergency rationing systems tested and calibrated. Governance structures at full operational load.

---

## Track E — Recruitment and Pre-Enrollment

Track E has three windows. Each window has hard prerequisites. The founding team may not advance to the next window until all prerequisites for that window are confirmed.

**Window 1 — Awareness (opens Month 4, parallel with C0):**

This window is information only. No commitments are solicited or accepted.

- Outreach describes the project's existence, purpose, and timeline
- VPCP-001 populations may be reached through partner organizations (shelters, VA offices, reentry programs, foster care agencies) with explicit messaging that no offer is being made and no spots are available
- Prohibited in Window 1: collecting identifying information beyond anonymous interest registration; implying guaranteed spots; suggesting urgency; accepting any expression of preference that could be construed as a commitment

**Window 2 — Pre-Enrollment (opens after Gate A / A2 clears, Month 8+):**

Prerequisites before Window 2 may open:
- Founding Legitimacy Dossier Gate A must be at PRODUCED status
- VPCP-008 prerequisites must be met: ICA staffed, exit support fund pre-capitalized, transitional support infrastructure operational
- INV-LAUNCH-1 preconditions (FC-YT1, FC-YT2) must be confirmed by the Ombuds

Pre-enrollment is a statement of intent, not a binding commitment. Residents may withdraw at any point without penalty, without explanation, and without consequence to any future application.

VPCP-scope residents may not enter pre-enrollment without completing VPCP-002 materials and an initial ICA session. No commitment documents may be signed in Window 2.

**Window 3 — Enrollment (opens after D2, Month 26–30):**

Prerequisites before Window 3 may open:
- First residential units complete and confirmed habitable
- All Window 2 prerequisites remain in force
- INV-LAUNCH-1 must be cleared by the Ombuds before the first resident takes occupancy

Cohort 1 target: 100–150 people. Recommended composition:
- 40–50% skilled and innovative volunteers
- 40–50% VPCP-scope (people experiencing homelessness, unemployment, justice system exit, or similar)
- 10% mixed (families, elders, children, medical needs)

Full-age-range occupancy planning is required. Pediatric healthcare, elder support, and school-age education must all be at minimum viable operation before any family with children enrolls. This is not a soft guideline — it is a prerequisite for Cohort 1 family enrollment.

---

## Gate Dependencies (Summary Table)

| Gate | Track | Prerequisite Gates | What It Unlocks |
|------|-------|--------------------|-----------------|
| A0 | A | none | B0, E Window 1 (information only) |
| A1 | A | A0 (60-day window complete) | Artifact production begins |
| A2 | A | A1 (all 7 artifacts PRODUCED) | B2, E Window 2, construction financing draws |
| B2 | B | A2, B1 | C1, D0 preparation |
| C2 | C | B2, C1 | D0 |
| D2 | D | C2, D1 | E Window 3 (enrollment) |
| INV-LAUNCH-1 cleared | — | D2 + Ombuds verification | First resident occupancy |

Gates A2, D2, and INV-LAUNCH-1 are critical-path gates. They may not be declared complete based on projected completion — only on confirmed, documented, independently verifiable status.

---

## Pre-Enrollment Obligations (VPCP-scope residents)

Before a VPCP-scope resident may enter Window 3 enrollment and sign any commitment document, all of the following must be complete:

1. VPCP-002 materials produced and delivered: 90-day transitional support arranged, benefits impact assessment complete, three alternatives to enrollment identified and documented
2. ICA initial session complete
3. 30-day cooling-off period complete (counted from ICA initial session, not from interest registration)
4. Understanding verification (teach-back) complete and recorded
5. No commitment documents may be signed in Window 2 — only in Window 3 after all of the above are confirmed

The ICA is responsible for verifying and documenting items 1–4 before any commitment document is presented. The founding team may not present commitment documents to VPCP-scope residents; the ICA presents them.

---

## Phased Occupancy Sequence

**Cohort 1 (D2 gate, Month 26–30):** 100–150 residents. Focus: operational testing of all core systems, oracle calibration at small scale, Tier 0 and Tier 1 onboarding, and VPCP-scope integration. Governance is in founding-team mode with resident advisory input. Post-activation audit (A4) completes during this phase.

**Cohort 2 (D3 gate, Month 36–42):** 300–500 residents. Full population mix. Agricultural and production zones operational and producing. The earned income economy begins. Governance transitions from founding-team mode toward constitutional governance as resident numbers allow.

**Cohort 3+ (D4 gate, Month 48–60):** Scale to 1,000+ residents. Shared Storehouse and emergency rationing systems fully operational and tested. Governance structures at full constitutional operation. Founder sunset (A3) has completed.

---

## Failure Contingency

If the pilot must wind down at any phase, the following apply without exception:

- Wind-down reserve (Founding Capital Framework FC-12) is activated immediately upon wind-down decision
- The Resident Transition Protocol (drafted before Cohort 1 arrives) governs housing continuity, healthcare continuity, data export rights, and transition support
- No resident loses Essential Access as a result of wind-down; the named alternative EA provider required by the Town Wind-Down Protocol activates before any resident loses access
- The founding team publishes a public post-mortem within 90 days of the wind-down decision, documenting what failed and what was learned

The Resident Transition Protocol is a prerequisite for Cohort 1 enrollment — it must be drafted and reviewed by the ICA before the first resident takes occupancy, not after.

---

## Long-Arc Alignment

The honest timeline from first public consultation to full-scale operation at 1,000+ residents is 9–12 years. This is not a startup timeline and should not be treated as one.

The founding team's job is not to predict what Cohort 1 will encounter. It is to create conditions in which Cohort 1 can encounter unexpected things, adapt, and report honestly. Governance documents will be amended under the constitutional amendment process. Systems will be recalibrated. Some things that seem well-designed now will prove wrong in practice.

The timeline depends on factors outside the founding team's control: regulatory approval timelines, site availability and negotiation, funder patience, and the political environment for experimental communities. If any track falls behind schedule, the critical-path gates — A2, D2, and INV-LAUNCH-1 — must not be rushed to compensate. Delay is preferable to premature occupancy. A community that opens before it is ready is harder to correct than a community that opens late.

The founding team commits to publishing an annual timeline update, noting where each track stands against these estimates and explaining any delays honestly.

docs.title: Founding Capital Framework
docs.summary: Constitutional instruments covered: Flow (market money), Essential Access (survival floor), Voice (civic weight), Service Record (contribution history), Shared Storehouse (emergency rationing).
docs.content:
# Founding Capital Framework

**Status:** Reference — Founding Phase

**Scope:** Defines how the pilot town (1–5,000 people, built from scratch) raises, holds, and disburses its founding capital. Applies from first fundraising contact through Year 5 post-activation.

**Constitutional instruments covered:** Flow (market money), Essential Access (survival floor), Voice (civic weight), Service Record (contribution history), Shared Storehouse (emergency rationing).

---

### FC-1 — Phase Capital Targets

- Phase 1 (land acquisition + infrastructure design): $15–22M
- Phase 2 (core infrastructure + first residential units): $35–55M
- Phase 3 (full buildout to 1,000+ residents): $60–90M

These ranges assume a US rural/peri-urban site with favorable land cost. Actual targets must be set by an independent financial review before Gate A (Founding Legitimacy Dossier). The financial review is conducted by a firm with no existing relationship to the founding team or any committed funder.

---

### FC-2 — Concentration Limits

No single funder — individual, foundation, government agency, or corporation — may contribute more than 20% of total committed capital across all phases.

No single funder may contribute more than 30% of capital in any single phase.

These limits are calculated on committed capital at the time of each funding agreement, not at financial close.

If a funder's share would exceed the limit because a subsequent funder withdraws, the Capital Steward must identify replacement funding or reduce scope before drawing on the oversized funder's capital. The Capital Steward does not disburse against a concentration-limit breach.

---

### FC-3 — Capital Steward

All founding capital is held by an independent Capital Steward entity — a nonprofit fiduciary or trust company with no financial interest in the pilot's success or failure.

The Capital Steward is not appointed by the founding team. It is selected by a process approved by the adversarial panel member named in the Founding Legitimacy Dossier.

The Capital Steward disburses capital only against approved construction milestones and operational budgets reviewed by the keyholder council. Disbursement requests not accompanied by a milestone completion record are declined.

The Capital Steward publishes monthly disbursement reports as publicly accessible documents. Reports include: amounts disbursed, milestone tied to each disbursement, remaining phase capital, and contingency reserve balance.

---

### FC-4 — Constitutional Primacy Clause

Every funding agreement must contain language substantially as follows:

> "This agreement is subordinate to the Humane Constitution and its implementing documents as amended from time to time by the governing keyholder council. In any conflict between this agreement and the Constitution, the Constitution governs. Funder's contractual rights do not include the right to override, suspend, or modify constitutional protections for residents."

This clause is non-negotiable. If a funder will not sign an agreement containing this clause, the funding is declined. The Capital Steward does not accept funds from an agreement that omits or qualifies this clause.

---

### FC-5 — Government Funding Walk-Away Rule

If a government agency — federal, state, county, or municipal — conditions its funding on the right to:

- Access resident data without individual consent
- Override constitutional protections for any resident population
- Require the pilot to cooperate with immigration enforcement
- Modify the Essential Access floor or exit rights

...then the founding team must decline the funding. The refusal and its basis are documented and published within 30 days of the refusal.

A government agency that will not sign a non-interference acknowledgment does not receive a funding agreement. The non-interference acknowledgment states that the agency's funding relationship does not alter the pilot's obligations to residents under the Humane Constitution.

This rule applies regardless of funding amount. There is no waiver process.

---

### FC-6 — Contingency Reserve

A contingency reserve equal to 25% of each phase's capital must be held in escrow before construction begins for that phase.

The reserve may only be drawn with joint approval of the Capital Steward and the keyholder council. A single-party draw request is not executed.

If the reserve drops below 15% of phase capital, construction in that phase pauses until the reserve is restored to 25%. The pause applies to all new disbursements; existing committed subcontracts are not disrupted mid-delivery.

---

### FC-7 — Pre-Activation Flow Issuance

Construction workers and other paid contributors during the founding period are paid in both external currency (for external expenses) and in Flow (for internal ledger credit).

The Flow portion of construction payroll qualifies as productive commitment under the constitutional Flow issuance model, shortening the path to Flow self-sufficiency.

The Flow exchange rate for construction payroll is set by the keyholder council, not the founding team. The rate is published before any construction payroll is issued. Mid-phase rate changes require keyholder council approval and are applied prospectively, not retroactively.

---

### FC-8 — Community Land Trust Structure

The pilot site is held in a Community Land Trust (CLT) structure: a nonprofit trust holds the land in perpetuity; residents hold ground leases.

The CLT governing board must include resident representatives at a minimum of 30% of board seats from the first cohort forward. No funder holds a CLT board seat.

The CLT articles of incorporation require that the land cannot be sold, developed contrary to the constitutional purpose, or encumbered by debt without a 60% resident vote and keyholder council approval.

No funder receives naming rights, equity, or reversionary interest in the land. Any agreement purporting to grant such interests is void and not executed by the Capital Steward.

---

### FC-9 — Naming and Influence Prohibitions

No facility, street, neighborhood, or community institution may be named after a funder without a community vote conducted under the pilot's Voice instrument.

No funder may hold a seat on the keyholder council, the founding team, or the Capital Steward board.

No funder may designate a specific resident or employee of the pilot as a condition of funding.

Violation of any of these prohibitions triggers FC-2 recalculation: the violating funder's contribution is recalculated at 0% for future phases, and the founding team must identify replacement capital before the next phase begins.

---

### FC-10 — Earned Income and Internal Economy

The pilot must develop a plan for earned income sufficient to cover ongoing operational costs by Year 5 post-activation.

Earned income sources include: the internal Flow economy, local agricultural and manufacturing production, service contracts with external parties, and housing fees structured as ground-lease payments to the CLT.

The Year 5 earned income plan is published and reviewed by the keyholder council before Gate A. The plan includes: projected revenue by source, assumptions for each projection, and the conditions under which each source fails.

---

### FC-11 — Financial Transparency

Full financial statements — income, expenses, capital accounts, contingency reserve balance — are published quarterly. Statements are published within 45 days of each quarter's end.

All funding agreements are public documents. Personal data of individual donors (home address, tax ID) may be redacted; funding amount, funder name, and constitutional primacy clause are not redacted.

Any funder conversation in which constitutional primacy (FC-4) or the walk-away rule (FC-5) was invoked is documented and published within 30 days of that conversation. Documentation includes: the date, the funder, the condition offered, and the founding team's response.

---

### FC-12 — Failure and Wind-Down Reserve

Before Cohort 1 arrives, a wind-down reserve equal to 6 months of Essential Access delivery for the expected first cohort must be pre-funded and held by the Capital Steward.

This reserve is drawn only in the event of pilot wind-down. It is used exclusively for resident transition support: temporary housing bridge, healthcare continuity, and Service Record export to a format usable outside the pilot.

The wind-down reserve is a separate account from the contingency reserve (FC-6). Draws against one account do not affect the other. The wind-down reserve is not available for construction cost overruns.

---

## Honest Limitations

This framework does not fully solve three problems:

**1. Informal funder influence not captured in funding agreements.** Concentration limits and naming prohibitions apply to formal contractual relationships. A funder with 18% of capital can still cultivate informal relationships with founding team members, provide in-kind services that create dependency, or set conditions in side communications that never appear in the agreement. The framework has no mechanism for detecting or disciplining influence that operates outside documented channels.

**2. Concentration limit evasion through affiliated entities.** FC-2 calculates concentration on committed capital per named funder. A funder with affiliated foundations, family offices, or corporate subsidiaries can route contributions through multiple entities to stay below the per-funder threshold while controlling a larger share of total capital. The framework requires a good-faith affiliation disclosure from each funder, but it has no enforcement mechanism beyond that disclosure.

**3. Government regulatory authority that operates independently of the funding relationship.** FC-5 blocks government funders who condition funding on constitutional violations. It does not block a government agency from exercising regulatory authority — zoning, health, building code, tax — in ways that constrain the pilot's constitutional choices without triggering the funding walk-away rule. A state agency could decline to fund the pilot while simultaneously using licensing authority to require resident data disclosure or to restrict Essential Access eligibility. The framework has no answer to regulatory coercion that arrives through channels other than the funding agreement.

docs.title: Founding Team Composition Standard
docs.summary: This document defines who may serve on the founding team, what disqualifies them, how the constitutional keyholder council is structured, and how founding team authority ends. The pilot town is a small built-from-scratch community (target: 1–5,000 residents). That means the founding team has enormous informal power during construction and early operation — power that does not appear in any org chart and is not checked by any election. This document exists to constrain that power structurally, not just rhetorically.
docs.content:
# Founding Team Composition Standard

**Status:** Active — Governance
**Scope:** Applies to the founding team of any Twelve Pillar Protocol pilot town during pre-activation and the founder sunset period.

This document defines who may serve on the founding team, what disqualifies them, how the constitutional keyholder council is structured, and how founding team authority ends. The pilot town is a small built-from-scratch community (target: 1–5,000 residents). That means the founding team has enormous informal power during construction and early operation — power that does not appear in any org chart and is not checked by any election. This document exists to constrain that power structurally, not just rhetorically.

The failure mode this document guards against: a well-intentioned founding team that gradually becomes the permanent informal government of the place it built, answering to no one because it built the systems and knows all the people.

---

### FT-1 — Composition Floor

The founding team must include at minimum one member with professional competency in each of the following domains: public health, legal aid, social work or case management, construction or land development, and community finance or cooperative economics. Competency is demonstrated through at least three years of paid or credentialed work in the domain, not through self-assessment alone. The adversarial panel member verifies competency claims before Gate A clears.

At least one founding team member must have personally experienced housing insecurity, incarceration, or another category defined in VPCP-001 (Vulnerable Population Consent Protocol). This is not a diversity checkbox — it is a structural epistemic requirement. The founding team will make decisions that affect people whose lives most of the team has never lived. One person who has lived those lives does not fix the knowledge gap, but their absence guarantees the team will miss things that matter.

At least one founding team member must hold no financial interest in the pilot's success beyond their own labor compensation. This means: not an equity holder, not a donor who receives naming rights, not a future resident whose property value is tied to the pilot's performance. This person is the internal skeptic. Their job is not to slow things down — it is to notice when the team is rationalizing.

No more than 40% of founding team members may share a single professional background or a single institutional affiliation. This rule exists because founding teams assembled from one network tend to share one set of blind spots. A team of housing researchers, a team of tech entrepreneurs, and a team of faith community organizers will each miss different things. The 40% cap forces composition diversity that makes those blind spots harder to sustain.

---

### FT-2 — Disqualifying Conflicts

A person may not serve on the founding team if any of the following apply at the time of appointment or arise during their tenure:

**Vendor equity.** They hold an equity or profit interest in any vendor expected to receive more than $100,000 in contracts from the pilot. The threshold is cumulative across related entities. If a vendor relationship develops after appointment, the founding team member has 30 days to divest or resign. "Expected to receive" includes contracts not yet signed if negotiations are underway.

**Family concentration.** They have a first- or second-degree family relationship (parent, child, sibling, grandparent, grandchild, aunt, uncle, niece, nephew, or equivalent by marriage) with another founding team member, unless the relationship is disclosed in writing and reviewed by a civil-society reviewer unaffiliated with the pilot. Disclosure does not automatically clear the conflict — the reviewer may require structural separation of responsibilities. This rule exists because family networks create informal authority structures that are nearly impossible to challenge from outside.

**Regulatory employment.** They are currently employed by a government agency with regulatory authority over the pilot site — zoning, environmental permitting, health licensing, or equivalent. The conflict here is not corruption; it is dual accountability that neither role can honor well.

**Financial crimes or fiduciary breaches.** They have a history of financial crimes or documented fiduciary breaches within the last 10 years. Self-disclosure is required at application. An independent background check is also required. A finding does not automatically disqualify — the review process examines nature, severity, and remediation. But non-disclosure of a known history is automatic disqualification.

---

### FT-3 — Keyholder Seat Allocation (9 Seats)

The constitutional keyholder council for the pilot operates on a 9-of-9 structure with a 7-of-9 threshold for Tier 1 constitutional changes. Seats are allocated as follows.

No founding team member may permanently occupy a keyholder seat after the founder sunset date (defined in FS-1). Founding team members may hold seats temporarily during the pre-activation phase only. All seats must transition to nominated or elected holders before the first resident cohort reaches 90 days of residency.

**Seat 1–2: Resident Elected.** Nominated and elected by residents with 90 or more days of continuous residency. The founding team has no role in the nomination or election process beyond administering the logistics if no other administrator is available — and in that case, a civil-society reviewer must observe the process. These seats represent the community's direct voice on constitutional matters.

**Seat 3–4: External Civil Society.** Nominated by organizations with no financial interest in the pilot. Nominees must not be former founding team members. "External" means the nominating organization operates independently of the pilot's governance and has no contractual relationship with its funders. These seats are designed to represent interests the community itself may not yet have organized around.

**Seat 5: Adversarial Panel Representative.** Nominated by the adversarial panel defined in the Founding Legitimacy Dossier. This seat exists to give the pilot's most persistent external critics a voice in its constitutional decisions. The adversarial panel representative is not required to support the pilot — they are required to apply their critical lens honestly.

**Seat 6: Legal/Constitutional.** Nominated by a legal aid or public defender organization in the region. The nominating organization must have a primary mission of serving low-income clients, not commercial legal practice. This seat ensures the council includes someone whose professional formation is built around rights protection rather than institutional administration.

**Seat 7: Public Health and Social Services.** Nominated by a public health body or social services organization in the region. The nominating organization must not be a funder or contractor of the pilot.

**Seat 8: Financial/Operations.** Nominated by a worker-owned cooperative or union organization. This seat ensures the council includes someone with accountability structures different from employer-employee and investor-portfolio relationships.

**Seat 9: Resident Lottery.** Selected by random lottery from residents with 18 or more months of continuous residency. The lottery is administered publicly. No founding team member may influence the pool or the selection process. This seat exists because elections favor residents who are already visible and socially connected; the lottery surfaces residents who may never be elected but have lived the community's realities longer than most.

---

### FT-4 — Nomination Process for Seats 3–8

Nominating organizations must be identified and approached before the Founding Legitimacy Dossier's Gate A clears. This is not a post-activation task. If the founding team has not identified candidate nominating organizations by Gate A, Gate A does not clear.

Nominating organizations may not be selected by the founding team alone. Each selection must be approved by the adversarial panel member. This approval is not a rubber stamp — the adversarial panel member may reject an organization if it has undisclosed ties to the pilot's funders, if its leadership includes former founding team members, or if its independence cannot be verified.

Each nominating organization must confirm in writing that it has no financial relationship with the pilot or its funders — no grants received, no contracts pending, no gifts accepted, no board membership overlap. This confirmation is public.

If a nominating organization cannot be identified for any seat within 60 days of Gate A, that seat defaults to a resident election using the same 90-day residency requirement as Seats 1–2. This default is not a failure mode — it is a safety valve. A pilot with strong resident participation may prefer resident elections over external nominations. What is not acceptable is a vacant seat.

---

### KH-1 — Keyholder Independence Test

Before any keyholder vote on a Tier 1 matter, the adversarial panel member must run an independence check. The check asks: are any two keyholders connected through shared employer, shared funder, family relationship, or documented political coordination within the past 24 months?

If yes, the affected keyholder must recuse from the vote, or the vote is delayed 30 days for a public disclosure period. During the disclosure period, the nature of the connection is published and residents may comment. After 30 days, the vote proceeds with the affected keyholder recusing or, if the keyholder disputes the independence finding, with a ruling from the adversarial panel member that is final.

The independence test is not about suspicion of bad faith. Keyholders from similar networks may share values without coordinating. But Tier 1 constitutional decisions require that the council not function as a single bloc. The test is structural, not accusatory.

---

### KH-2 — Keyholder Rotation

Each keyholder seat has a maximum 4-year term. Terms are staggered at initial formation so that no more than three seats turn over in any 18-month period.

Seats 1–2 (Resident Elected) and Seat 9 (Resident Lottery) are renewable. Residents elect or re-draw from the eligible pool at the end of each term. There is no term limit on these seats beyond the structural checks above.

Seats 3–8 (Nominated) rotate to new nominating organizations after each term. The same individual may not be renominated by a new organization as a mechanism for extending their tenure — if a person has served one full term in any nominated seat, they are ineligible for any other nominated seat for 4 years following the end of their term.

---

### KH-3 — Keyholder Disclosure

All keyholders are disclosed publicly: their name, their nominating organization or selection method, any conflicts of interest they disclosed at the time of appointment, and any conflicts that arose and were reviewed during their tenure. This disclosure is maintained in the pilot's public governance record and updated within 14 days of any change.

---

### KH-4 — Disqualifying Conflicts During Tenure

A keyholder who develops a disqualifying conflict — defined by the same standards as FT-2 with adjustments for the keyholder role — must recuse from affected votes within 7 days of discovering the conflict and must either resolve the conflict or vacate the seat within 14 days. If they do not vacate, the adversarial panel member initiates a removal review. Removal requires a 6-of-9 keyholder vote, excluding the affected keyholder.

---

### KH-5 — Meeting Records

Keyholder meeting records — including attendance, agenda items, votes cast, and dissenting statements — are public within 7 days of each meeting. Summary publication is not sufficient; the full record is published. Closed-session discussions are permitted only for matters involving resident privacy, ongoing legal proceedings, or security vulnerabilities. The existence of a closed session and its general subject must still be disclosed within 7 days.

---

### FS-1 — Founder Sunset Date

The founder sunset date is the earlier of: (a) 90 days after Cohort 1 reaches full occupancy as defined in the pilot's occupancy agreement, or (b) 24 months after the first permanent resident arrives. Whichever date comes first is the sunset date. The date is calculated publicly and announced no later than 30 days before it arrives.

---

### FS-2 — Loss of Constitutional Authority

On the sunset date, all founding team members lose any constitutional authority, appointment power, or keyholder position they hold by virtue of being founders. This loss is automatic and does not require a vote. If a founding team member is also a resident-elected or lottery-selected keyholder at the sunset date, they retain that seat — because they hold it as a resident, not as a founder. Any seat they hold by founding team appointment is vacated on the sunset date and filled through the standard nomination or election process.

---

### FS-3 — Post-Sunset Presence

Founding team members may remain in the community after the sunset date as residents, employees of community organizations, or contractors to the pilot's governance bodies. What they may not do is hold any governance position — elected, appointed, or advisory — for 24 months following the sunset date, unless they are elected by current residents through a standard election with no preferential treatment based on their founding role.

The 24-month restriction applies to formal and informal governance roles. If a founding team member is functioning as a de facto decision-maker — consulted routinely before major decisions, present in keyholder meetings as an advisor, directing staff who implement governance decisions — that is a governance role and triggers the restriction regardless of what it is called.

---

### FS-4 — Sunset Report

Each founding team member must produce or co-produce a public sunset report before the sunset date. The report documents: what the founding team built; what they attempted and failed; what decisions they made that residents later challenged or reversed; what informal authority the founding team member currently holds and is actively releasing on the sunset date; and what they would do differently.

The sunset report is not a success narrative. A sunset report that contains no failures, no mistakes, and no honest accounting of informal authority is rejected by the adversarial panel member and must be revised before the sunset date.

---

### FS-5 — Adversarial Panel Review of Sunset Report

The sunset report must be submitted to the adversarial panel member no later than 45 days before the sunset date. The adversarial panel member has 30 days to respond with findings. If the report is rejected, the founding team member has 15 days to revise and resubmit. If the revised report is also rejected, the sunset proceeds but the panel member's findings are published alongside the report.

---

### FS-6 — Post-Sunset Governance Re-entry

Any founding team member who attempts to re-enter governance before the 24-month post-sunset period ends — through any formal or informal channel — triggers an automatic Perpetual Humility Review as defined below. The review is triggered by the act of re-entry, not by its intent. Good intentions do not pause the review.

---

### FS-7 — Financial Benefit from Authorship

Founding team members who profit financially from authorship of the pilot's narrative — books, speaking engagements, consulting contracts that trade on their founding role, documentary participation fees, and equivalent — redirect 50% of that compensation to the communal fund for 5 years following the sunset date. This obligation is disclosed to all founding team members before the pilot begins and acknowledged in writing as a condition of joining the founding team.

The rationale: the founding team will be able to tell the story of this place for the rest of their lives, in ways residents cannot. Residents do not receive speaking fees for living through what the founding team built. The 50% redirect is not punishment — it is a recognition that narrative profit from this community belongs partly to the community.

---

### CS-1 — Resident Notice for Founding Period Decisions

During the founding period (pre-sunset), founding team decisions that affect residents require advance notice to residents of at least 7 days and a public comment period of at least 5 days. Notice must be delivered through channels accessible to all residents — not only those who attend founding team meetings or follow digital communication.

Decisions that are not prospective — emergency responses to safety risks, time-sensitive legal filings, and equivalent — are exempt from the advance notice requirement but must be disclosed within 48 hours of being made, with an explanation of why advance notice was not possible.

---

### CS-2 — Resident Opposition Review

Any founding team decision opposed by more than 30% of current residents — measured by a formal expression of opposition through a process defined in advance, not by the founding team's informal read of the room — must be reviewed by the adversarial panel member before taking effect. The review is not a veto. The panel member may clear the decision, require modifications, or recommend a resident vote. Their recommendation is published. If the founding team proceeds against a recommendation to halt or modify, that decision is logged as a contested action in the public governance record.

---

### CS-3 — Founding Team Compensation

Founding team compensation is public. Individual compensation is disclosed in the monthly status report (CS-4). No founding team member's total compensation — including salary, equity equivalents, housing subsidies tied to the role, and other benefits — may exceed 150% of the median resident compensation for the same period. If the pilot has not yet established resident compensation benchmarks, the founding team uses regional median income as the baseline.

This rule exists because founding teams that pay themselves well above what residents earn gradually lose the capacity to understand what scarcity feels like in the community they govern. That is not a moral claim — it is an empirical one.

---

### CS-4 — Monthly Status Report

The founding team publishes a monthly status report covering: finances (income, expenditures, outstanding obligations, and variance from budget); construction and infrastructure progress; resident wellbeing indicators as defined in the Vulnerable Population Consent Protocol; and consent health (whether the community's ongoing consent processes are functioning, what has been challenged, and what has been resolved). The report is public and written in plain language accessible to residents without professional backgrounds in finance or governance.

---

### CS-5 — Founding Team Removal

The founding team may be removed in whole or in part by a 60% vote of current residents at any time during the founding period. The vote is initiated by a petition signed by 25% of residents. The petition triggers a 14-day deliberation period during which the founding team may respond publicly. After 14 days, the vote is held. If 60% of residents vote to remove, removal takes effect within 30 days, during which time the adversarial panel member oversees a transition to interim governance.

---

### Perpetual Humility Review

The Perpetual Humility Review is triggered by FS-6 (founding team member attempts governance re-entry before the 24-month post-sunset restriction ends) or by a resident petition signed by 20% of current residents at any time post-sunset.

The review is conducted by the adversarial panel member. It begins with the founding team reading aloud — collectively, or individually if collective reading is not possible — the Humility Declaration:

> "We, the founding team, built what we could. We did not build what was needed. We brought our best intentions and our worst blind spots. We made decisions that affected people whose lives we could not fully understand. We didn't figure out shit. That is why every check in this document exists."

This declaration is not a ritual of humiliation. It is a structural reset — a requirement that the people who built this place acknowledge, in front of the people who live in it, that building something does not make you its authority.

After the declaration, the review evaluates three questions:

**(a) Informal authority re-entry.** Does any founding team member currently hold informal authority that has re-entered governance in practice — through advisory roles, staff relationships, financial leverage, or social influence that functions as decision-making power?

**(b) Resident capacity to challenge.** Are there residents who feel unable to challenge founding team members, contest founding team decisions, or raise grievances without social or economic cost? The adversarial panel member interviews a sample of residents, including recent arrivals, to assess this.

**(c) Corrective actions required.** Based on findings from (a) and (b), what structural changes — in roles, relationships, compensation, or governance design — are required? The panel member's corrective action recommendations are binding unless overturned by a 7-of-9 keyholder vote.

The Perpetual Humility Review recurs every 3 years post-sunset for as long as any founding team member lives in or works with the community in any capacity. The recurrence is not contingent on problems being found. It is contingent on founding team members remaining present. The founding team's presence is the reason for the review.

headings.text: FS-5 — Adversarial Panel Review of Sunset Report
